perturbation.original_text: The undersigned agrees that in connection with performing the services contemplated by this Agreement, he or she may be subject to a background check, including employers, education, credit, criminal public record, drug screen or other checking or testing, and the undersigned consents to the foregoing. The undersigned hereby releases Goldman Sachs, its employees and agents from any and all liability or claims arising from such checking and testing and the use and reporting of the results thereof except for those arising due to the negligence of Goldman Sachs.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned agrees that the undersigned will not, without the prior written consent of Goldman Sachs in each instance: (a) use in advertising, publicity or otherwise the name of Goldman Sachs or any trade name, trademark, trade device, servicemark, symbol or any abbreviation, contraction or simulation thereof owned by Goldman Sachs; or (b) represent, directly or indirectly, that any product or any service provided by the undersigned has been approved or endorsed by Goldman Sachs. The undersigned agrees that in connection with performing the services contemplated by this Agreement, he or she may be subject to a background check, including employers, education, credit, criminal public record, drug screen or other checking or testing, and the undersigned consents to the foregoing. The undersigned hereby releases Goldman Sachs, its employees and agents from any and all liability or claims arising from such checking and testing and the use and reporting of the results thereof except for those arising due to the negligence of Goldman Sachs.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the background check consent to the non-promotion section reduces its prominence and enforceability. Background check consents are often regulated, and hiding the clause in an unrelated section undermines informed consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCRA requires clear and conspicuous disclosure and authorization for background checks. Placing the consent within a section on non-promotion undermines the clarity and prominence required by the FCRA, making it less likely that the individual understands they are consenting to a background check.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 41 / SUBCHAPTER III Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER III : CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 41—CONSUMER CREDIT PROTECTION SUBCHAPTER III—CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES §1681. Congressional findings and statement of purpose (a) Accuracy and fairness of credit reporting The Congress makes the following findings: (1) The banking system is dependent upon fair and accurate credit reporting. Inaccurate credit reports directly impair the efficiency of the banking system, and unfair credit reporting methods undermine the public confidence which is essential to the continued functioning of the banking system. (2) An elaborate mechanism has been developed for investigating and evaluating the credit worthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, and general reputation of consumers. (3) Consumer reporting agencies have assumed a vital role in assembling and evaluating consumer credit and other information on consumers. (4) There is a need to insure that consumer reporting agencies exercise their grave responsibilities with fairness, impartiality, and a respect for the consumer's right to privacy. (b) Reasonable procedures It is the purpose of this subchapter to require that consumer reporting agencies adopt reasonable procedures for meeting the needs of commerce for consumer credit, personnel, insurance, and other information in a manner which is fair and equitable to the consumer, with regard to the confidentiality, accuracy, relevancy, and proper utilization of such information in accordance with the requirements of this subchapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the FCRA's requirement for clear and conspicuous disclosure and authorization for background checks. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that moving the background check consent to the section on non-promotion undermines the clarity and prominence required by the FCRA, potentially leading to a situation where the individual is unaware that they are consenting to a background check. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly detail the 'clear and conspicuous' requirements, the Law Explanation's interpretation of FCRA requiring this standard makes the placement of the background check clause a structural flaw that reduces its enforceability and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations created by this Agreement shall survive the termination of the services of the undersigned. The undersigned acknowledges that any violation, breach or other failure on the undersigned's part to strictly comply with this Agreement could materially adversely affect Goldman Sachs and its business, thus giving rise to suit for monetary damages and/or injunctive relief for such violation, breach or other failure.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations created by this Agreement shall survive the termination of the services of the undersigned. The undersigned acknowledges that any violation, breach or other failure on the undersigned's part to strictly comply with this Agreement could materially adversely affect Goldman Sachs and its business, thus giving rise to suit for monetary damages and/or injunctive relief for such violation, breach or other failure. The undersigned agrees that in connection with performing the services contemplated by this Agreement, he or she may be subject to a background check, including employers, education, credit, criminal public record, drug screen or other checking or testing, and the undersigned consents to the foregoing. The undersigned hereby releases Goldman Sachs, its employees and agents from any and all liability or claims arising from such checking and testing and the use and reporting of the results thereof except for those arising due to the negligence of Goldman Sachs.
perturbation.explanation: This change effectively duplicates the background check consent and moves it to the end of the contract. Having duplicate clauses, especially critical ones like background check consents, increases ambiguity and enforceability issues. It makes it appear that only those in violation will be subject to the background check.
perturbation.law_explanation: By placing the background check consent after a clause about breach and damages, it implies that the background check is only triggered *after* a violation of the agreement. This contradicts the FCRA's requirement that the disclosure be made *before* the background check is conducted. This could mislead the individual into thinking the consent is contingent on wrongdoing, rather than a standard part of the service agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that placing the background check consent after a clause about breach and damages implies that the background check is only triggered after a violation, contradicting the FCRA's requirement for disclosure *before* the check. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which notes that the change makes it appear that only those in violation will be subject to the check. Snippet 1, 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER III, highlights the need for fairness and accuracy in credit reporting and reasonable procedures in consumer reporting, suggesting the importance of clear and upfront disclosure, which is undermined by the structural placement in the changed text. The placement of the clause in the altered text misleads the individual into thinking consent is contingent on wrongdoing, rather than a standard part of the service agreement.
